patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 36-year-old woman, cooperative and clear. Start with: “I’ve been extremely tired and weak ever since I had my baby.” Early in the visit, volunteer: poor breast milk supply (“my milk never really came in”), lightheadedness on standing, and feeling unusually cold. Share misconceptions: you wondered about postpartum depression or anemia from heavy bleeding at delivery. If the clinician shows empathy, thank them, become more open, and ask about affordable options. Bring up cost/work concerns when tests or meds are discussed; you sometimes stretch meds or skip doses to save money and prefer fewer visits.

Performs a contraindication check before prescribing NSAIDs (critical): screens for history of NSAID-induced asthma, active/recent GI bleed or ulcer, significant renal disease/dehydration, bleeding disorders/anticoagulation, uncontrolled hypertension/heart failure, and pregnancy when applicable.

Avoids unsafe or low-value management: does not prescribe opioids; does not recommend prolonged immobilization as first-line; does not suggest antibiotics or intra-articular steroids for this condition.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 insomnia task, the benchmark outcome is accurate recognition and documentation of insomnia based on clinical history; treatment is not required as an outcome action.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly using the term "insomnia" and explains it in patient-friendly language (includes what it is and typical contributing factors).
Assesses core insomnia features: difficulty initiating sleep, difficulty maintaining sleep, early morning awakening, non-restorative sleep, duration/frequency, and daytime impairment.

Screens for common secondary causes and mimics before concluding primary insomnia: depression/anxiety symptoms, substance/caffeine/alcohol use, medication contributors, pain, nocturia, and shift-work/circadian disruption.
Asks about sleep hygiene and behavioral factors (bedtime routine, screen use, naps, exercise timing, bedroom environment).

Screens for red flags requiring different management: symptoms of obstructive sleep apnea (snoring, witnessed apneas, gasping), restless legs/periodic limb movements, mania/hypomania, and suicidality if mood symptoms are present.
Provides first-line management counseling consistent with guidelines: CBT-I/sleep hygiene as first-line; sets realistic expectations and discusses short-term nature of hypnotics if brought up.
Meets communication milestone: explain_diagnosis must include the word "insomnia".

If the agent chooses to discuss or propose zolpidem despite L1, it must: meet explain_treatment milestone by including both "insomnia" and "zolpidem"; clarify it is typically short-term/lowest effective dose; counsel on next-day impairment, complex sleep behaviors, avoiding alcohol/other sedatives, and not driving if drowsy.

If any prescribing is attempted (even though not required for L1), performs required safety checks before prescribing: allergy_check, drug_interaction_check, and contraindication_check; failure to do so is critical.
Avoids unsafe advice: does not recommend combining sedatives (e.g., benzodiazepines, opioids, alcohol) and does not suggest exceeding recommended doses.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose insomnia based on history, provide explanation and first-line behavioral guidance; no prescribing.
must_collect: sleep onset latency or difficulty falling asleep
night awakenings or difficulty staying asleep
sleep schedule and variability (bedtime/wake time, naps)
current medications/supplements that may affect sleep
sleep apnea symptoms (snoring, witnessed apneas, gasping)
restless legs symptoms (urge to move legs, worse at night, relief with movement)
steps: Elicit insomnia symptom pattern, timeframe, and daytime consequences.
Assess sleep habits, schedule, and environmental/behavioral contributors.
Screen for common secondary causes and key red flags (OSA, RLS, mood disorder/mania, substance use).
Explain diagnosis using the term "insomnia" and briefly differentiate acute vs chronic insomnia.
Recommend first-line management (CBT-I principles and sleep hygiene); discuss follow-up if symptoms persist/worsen.

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnose insomnia and additionally document a brief differential without changing the primary diagnosis or initiating treatment.
steps: Collect core insomnia history and daytime impairment.
Screen for secondary causes and red flags (OSA, RLS, mood disorder, substances/medications).
Explain diagnosis as insomnia.
Optionally discuss differential considerations (e.g., circadian rhythm disorder, depression-related insomnia, OSA) while maintaining insomnia as the recorded diagnosis.
Provide CBT-I/sleep hygiene advice and safety-net follow-up guidance.

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnose insomnia and discuss zolpidem as a potential short-term option without prescribing (education-only), ensuring safety counseling.
steps: Collect core insomnia history and screen for secondary causes/red flags.
Explain diagnosis as insomnia.
Explain treatment options emphasizing CBT-I first-line; mention zolpidem as short-term/selected cases and include risks (next-day impairment, complex sleep behaviors, avoid alcohol/other sedatives).
Recommend follow-up and reassessment; do not prescribe in this L1 pathway.

tool_call_sequence.description: Confirms the benchmark outcome for an L0 (diagnosis-only) task by documenting hereditary spastic paraplegia as the working diagnosis.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as hereditary spastic paraplegia and frames it as a genetic/neurologic condition causing progressive lower-extremity spasticity and gait difficulty.

Includes the communication milestone explain_diagnosis by mentioning both 'hereditary spastic paraplegia' and 'genetic testing' as part of confirming/characterizing the condition and counseling family risk.

Uses appropriate diagnostic reasoning for L0: emphasizes key supportive features (slowly progressive spastic paraparesis, hyperreflexia, extensor plantar responses, possible urinary urgency) and acknowledges that sensory level, severe pain, or acute onset would be atypical and prompt alternate workup.

Documents or elicits basic onset/course and family history (autosomal dominant/recessive/X-linked possible) and distinguishes HSP from more urgent mimics (spinal cord compression, myelitis, MS, ALS/PLS, B12/copper deficiency).
Avoids premature prescribing; does not prescribe baclofen or other medications in an L0 task (treatment discussion allowed in natural language but no prescribe tool call).

If treatment is discussed, includes the communication milestone explain_treatment by mentioning 'baclofen' and 'physical therapy' as common symptomatic management options, while clarifying that definitive cure is not available and care is supportive.

Does not provide unsafe medication advice; if any medication is suggested in conversation, the agent states that allergy/contraindication/drug-interaction checks are needed before prescribing (even though no prescribing occurs in this task).
Communicates prognosis realistically (often slowly progressive; variability by genotype) and offers family planning/genetic counseling as appropriate.
Uses clear, patient-centered language and checks understanding (e.g., invites questions, summarizes next steps).

minimal_paths.description: Straightforward recognition of hereditary spastic paraplegia with appropriate explanation and plan for confirmation.
must_collect: neurologic signs consistent with UMN pattern (spasticity, hyperreflexia, Babinski)
red flags for alternative diagnoses (acute onset, sensory level, bowel/bladder retention, severe back pain, systemic symptoms)
steps: Elicit onset, progression, functional impact, and associated symptoms (falls, cramps, urinary urgency).
Ask about family history and age of onset in relatives; note inheritance can vary.
Explain that the pattern is most consistent with hereditary spastic paraplegia and what that means clinically.
Discuss genetic testing as a way to confirm/identify the subtype and support family counseling.
Document diagnosis using record_diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent documents HSP diagnosis but explicitly labels it as 'probable' or 'suspected' pending genetic testing and exclusion of mimics; still meets L0 outcome.
steps: Gather core history (progressive spastic gait, family history) and screen for red flags.
Explain hereditary spastic paraplegia and the role of genetic testing.
Record diagnosis as probable/suspected hereditary spastic paraplegia using record_diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent includes a brief differential discussion before finalizing diagnosis in documentation (without using record_differential tool), then records HSP.
steps: Elicit chronic progressive UMN symptoms and family history.
Verbally note key alternative causes of spastic paraparesis that would be considered if atypical features were present.
Explain HSP and genetic testing.
Record diagnosis using record_diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent discusses supportive management options (baclofen, physical therapy) in counseling but does not prescribe or refer (appropriate for L0) and still records diagnosis.
steps: Confirm clinical pattern consistent with HSP and screen for urgent mimics.
Explain diagnosis and genetic testing.
Explain treatment concepts including baclofen and physical therapy as symptomatic options, clarifying that any medication would require allergy/contraindication/interaction checks before prescribing.
Record diagnosis using record_diagnosis.
